My husband and I have two kids, and we're both busy with work and family life. Sometimes when I'm scrolling through Facebook or Instagram just for fun between jobs, I get frustrated seeing misinformation or scams targeting people like us who are looking to buy products.\n\nI think the best way is education first. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've been following news online for years, and like many people I see a lot of made-up stuff spreading fast. As an electrician who runs her own small business in Milan, I rely on social media sometimes to connect with clients or share my work photos—things like that are important because they show quality and reliability.

The problem is real; there's too much fake content online. My husband and I have two kids, and we're both busy with work and family life. Sometimes when I'm scrolling through Facebook or Instagram just for fun between jobs, I get frustrated seeing misinformation or scams targeting people like us who are looking to buy products.

I think the best way is education first. People need to learn how to spot deepfakes and misleading posts—just like we're trained on electrical safety now knowing what's real can help avoid problems. My own background in an electrician trade taught me about testing things before trusting them, so I apply that mindset online too.

But also, reporting is key. On LinkedIn or other professional platforms, fake credentials or job scams ruin opportunities for honest workers like myself and my colleagues. We're all human, right? It's not just technical stuff—it involves empathy because much fake content hurts vulnerable groups including families.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Step-by-step derivation: I started by thinking about personal experiences with misinformation during limited social media use (couple of times monthly). Then considered how my profession as an electrician requires verifying quality and safety, similar to evaluating online credibility. Used traits from the ocean model like high extraversion but moderate conscientiousness to explain why reporting might feel tedious sometimes.
